title: Children Law
description: Private and public children law, welfare and section 8 orders.

topics.title: Children Act 1989: Core Principles
topics.description: Welfare, delay, parental responsibility and section 8 orders.

questions.q: What is the court's paramount consideration when determining a question about a child's upbringing?
questions.explain: Section 1 Children Act 1989 makes the child's welfare the court's paramount consideration.

questions.q: What does the 'no delay' principle mean in children proceedings?
options: Proceedings must always end within 7 days
Delay is likely to prejudice the child's welfare
The court must avoid expert evidence
questions.explain: Delay is treated as likely to prejudice the child's welfare.

questions.q: Which of these is a section 8 order?
questions.explain: Section 8 orders include child arrangements, prohibited steps and specific issue orders.

questions.q: What is the court's central consideration in a relocation application?
questions.explain: Relocation applications are determined by reference to the child's welfare.

questions.q: Is there an automatic presumption in favour of the primary carer's relocation proposal?
questions.explain: There is no automatic presumption. The court evaluates the welfare merits of the specific proposal.

topics.title: Public Law Children
topics.description: Threshold, care orders, supervision orders and local authority intervention.

questions.q: What must a local authority establish before the court can make a care or supervision order?
questions.explain: The section 31 threshold criteria must be crossed before a care or supervision order can be made.

questions.q: Which order gives the local authority parental responsibility?
questions.explain: A care order gives the local authority parental responsibility, shared with others who hold it.

title: Financial Remedies
description: Matrimonial finance, section 25, needs, sharing and pensions.

questions.q: Which case is strongly associated with the modern sharing principle?
questions.explain: White v White is a key authority on fairness and non-discrimination.

questions.q: What are the three main strands of fairness often cited from Miller; McFarlane?
questions.explain: Needs, compensation and sharing are the recognised fairness strands.

topics.title: Section 25 Factors
topics.description: Income, earning capacity, needs, standard of living, age and contributions.

questions.q: Which statute contains the section 25 financial remedy factors?
questions.explain: Section 25 is in the Matrimonial Causes Act 1973.

questions.q: How is conduct treated in financial remedies?
options: Usually only relevant where it would be inequitable to disregard it
questions.explain: Conduct has a limited role and is usually relevant only where inequitable to disregard.

title: TLATA
description: Trusts of land, co-ownership, beneficial interests and occupation disputes.

topics.title: TLATA Basics
topics.description: Trusts of Land and Appointment of Trustees Act 1996.

options: Trusts of land and co-ownership disputes
questions.explain: TLATA governs trusts of land and applications concerning ownership and sale of land.

questions.q: Which relief is commonly sought under TLATA?
options: Declaration of beneficial interests and/or order for sale
questions.explain: TLATA claims often seek declarations as to shares and orders for sale.

topics.title: Beneficial Interests
topics.description: Resulting trusts, constructive trusts and common intention.

questions.q: A common intention constructive trust usually requires common intention and what else?
questions.explain: The claimant usually needs to establish common intention and detrimental reliance.

questions.q: Which concept may arise where one party acts to their detriment relying on an assurance?
questions.explain: Proprietary estoppel can arise from assurance, reliance and detriment.

title: Domestic Abuse
description: Protective injunctions, occupation orders and urgent applications.

topics.title: Non-molestation and Occupation Orders
topics.description: Protective relief under the Family Law Act 1996.

questions.q: What does a non-molestation order generally prohibit?
questions.explain: It protects against molestation, harassment, threats and related conduct.

questions.q: Which order can regulate occupation of the family home?
questions.explain: Occupation orders regulate who may occupy or enter the home.

title: Procedure
description: Family Procedure Rules, directions, disclosure and hearings.

topics.title: FPR Basics
topics.description: Directions, bundles, position statements and disclosure.

questions.q: What is Form E primarily used for?
options: Financial disclosure in financial remedy proceedings
questions.explain: Form E is the principal financial disclosure document in financial remedy proceedings.

options: A concise document setting out a party's position for a hearing
questions.explain: Position statements summarise the party's position and orders sought.
